coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor for sensitive visualization of full-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic residues in the environment
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Ruijie
Li, Lingfei
Zhu, Yaowei
Zhan, Minghui
Zhao, Jun
Jiang, Changlong
Liu, Bianhua
coredata.dc:description: Misuse of fluoroquinolone antibiotics (FQs) leads to environmental residues that threaten human health and ecosystems. Therefore, the development of efficient and sensitive micro-residue detection techniques for FQs is crucial for the environment and human health. Here, we developed a functionalised ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive, rapid and intuitive detection and analysis of FQs residues in the environment. The sensor was constructed from green fluorescent carbon dots (GCDs) and red fluorescent europium metal–organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs). After the addition of FQs, the carboxyl group on the surface of FQs combined with the amino group on the surface of GCDs to form an amide bond, which was facilitated by the sensitisation effect and energy transfer, resulting in the fluorescence from green to red, and the detection limit of 4.3 nM, detection time within 1 s, which enables rapid micro-detection of FQs. Combined with 3D printing technology, we designed a hand-held fluorescent needle for direct quantification of FQs residues in real samples with a detection limit of 4.9 nM. This technology provides an innovative scientific approach to the rapid detection of trace levels of environmental contaminants, helping to reduce the health and environmental hazards of FQs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric Fluorescent Probe
Environmental Analysis
Sensitive
Visualization Detection
Fluoroquinolone Antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of FQs in real samples
A study of the design principle and mechanism of probes
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of FQs using GCDs@Eu-MOF probe
Detection of FQs in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000202), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085\u202FMB30), Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Agricultural Sensors of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, and Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment of Anhui Province. (KLAS2022KF001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of GCDs. (B) (C) SEM images of Eu-MOF. (D) UV-Vis absorption spectra of GCDs@Eu-MOF and FQs. (E) FT-IR spectra of GCDs, GCDs + FQs. (F) The XPS of GCDs@Eu-MOF (the inset shows the XPS Eu 3d). (G) XRD pattern and standardized spectrum of Eu-MOF. (H) Thermogravimetric analysis plot of Eu-MOF. (I) Nitrogen adsorption desorption curve of Eu-MOF, inset shows pore size distribution.

Fluorescence spectra of the probe solution upon the exposure of (A) SPX, (B) GAT, (C) LVX, (D) CIP, (E) FLE, (F) ENX, (G) PLX. (H) LOM, (I) MBF, the inset shows the corresponding fluorescence color photos under 320 nm UV irradiation.
Linear fit curves of fluorescence intensity I690/I500 for (A) SPX, (B) GAT, (C) LVX, (D) CIP, (E) FLE, (F) ENX, (G) PLX. (H) LOM, (I) MBF.

(A) The fluorescence intensity-ratio of the sample after the addition of 1.4 μM of various impurities. (B) Probe selectiveity and interference test. (C) Fluorescent photographs of the end of the reaction corresponding to each substance.

(A) Color variation of the handheld fluorescing probe needle during the determination of FQs (0–1.4 μM); (B) The mobile phone screen of the sensor platform; (C) The color variation of the rotating diaphragm observed by the smartphone sensor platform; (D) the correlation curve between the R/G and the concentration of FQs (0–1.4 μM).
Synthesis route of GCDs@Eu-MOF and mechanism diagram for detecting FQs.
Determination of FQs spiked in real samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor for sensitive visualization of full-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic residues in the environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230039, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, 230039, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Hefei, Anhui 230031, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Hefei, Anhui 230031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Sensors, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Anhui Provincial Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment, Anhui Agricultural University. Hefei, Anhui 230036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs
Anhui Provincial Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Agricultural Sensors, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Anhui Provincial Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment, Anhui Agricultural University. Hefei, Anhui, 230036, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Misuse of fluoroquinolone antibiotics (FQs) leads to environmental residues that threaten human health and ecosystems. Therefore, the development of efficient and sensitive micro-residue detection techniques for FQs is crucial for the environment and human health. Here, we developed a functionalised ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive, rapid and intuitive detection and analysis of FQs residues in the environment. The sensor was constructed from green fluorescent carbon dots (GCDs) and red fluorescent europium metal–organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs). After the addition of FQs, the carboxyl group on the surface of FQs combined with the amino group on the surface of GCDs to form an amide bond, which was facilitated by the sensitisation effect and energy transfer, resulting in the fluorescence from green to red, and the detection limit of 4.3 nM, detection time within 1 s, which enables rapid micro-detection of FQs. Combined with 3D printing technology, we designed a hand-held fluorescent needle for direct quantification of FQs residues in real samples with a detection limit of 4.9 nM. This technology provides an innovative scientific approach to the rapid detection of trace levels of environmental contaminants, helping to reduce the health and environmental hazards of FQs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoroquinolone antibiotics (FQs) represent a relatively recent addition to the class of synthetic antibiotics [1]. The antibiotic has broad-spectrum antimicrobial efficacy against all bacteria of opportunity [2]. These drugs are commonly employed for the treatment of infectious diseases in animals, including poultry and fish. It is important to note that residual antibiotics in foods of animal origin (e.g., honey, milk, and meat) however, can cause a number of sustainable harms. The emergence of antimicrobial resistance in patients, as well as the development of bacterial infections and gastrointestinal irritation, and inefficiencies in antibiotic therapy, are among the adverse effects associated with the use of antimicrobial agents [3]. The production and consumption of FQs is increasing due to their strong antimicrobial properties and wide range of application scenarios [4]. This has led to an increasing accumulation of antibiotics in food since 2000 [5]. Considering the rising consumption of quinolones and the adverse effects associated with their use, regulatory bodies maximal tolerated residue levels (MRLs) for quinolones in a wide range of foods of animal source, such as the European Union (EU) [6]. The EU has set maximum composition limits for the eight quinolones at 30–800 mg kg−1 [7]. The development of analytical techniques capable of monitoring and characterizing trace drug residues in biological samples and the environment is an important area of research [8].

To the present day, several methods of analysis have been developed for the identification of residues of FQs. These techniques fall into two main groups: one is an instrumental measurement and the other is an immunoassay. The main instrumental techniques are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], liquid chromatography-electrospray ionization-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-ESI-MS/MS) [10–12], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [13–15], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, and electrochemical method [16–19]. However, most of these methods have some unavoidable drawbacks, such as the need for expensive and complicated instruments, the unavoidable impact on the environment, and the use of materials that are not sufficiently environmentally friendly [20]. In response to such limitations, several researchers recently designed new nanomaterials for the detection of FQs to facilitate the diagnosis in clinical settings. For instance, Tang [21] et al. UCPs@Fe3O4@MIP synthesized a probe, which combines both magnetization and the molecular recognition based on upconverted particles on the nuclear shell layer. Additionally, Mao [22] et al. a silver nanocluster Cu2+ sensing system for trace detection of FQs was developed using its fluorescence switch-on property. However, many of these methods often require complex preparation processes due to binding to metal ions (e.g. La3+ and Eu3+) or molecularly imprinted polymers [23]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a convenient, sensitive, green, environmentally friendly, safety, selective and anti-interference method for the detection of FQs in food and environmental samples without causing harm to the environment.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have become a new nanomaterial of nanomaterials with a multitude of potential applications. These include applications in sensors, bio-Imaging, pharmaceutical drug release, micro-fluids, photocatalysis, luminescence and many others [24–27]. As the study of CDs progresses, the factors that contribute to their properties can be identified, including particle size distribution, superficial condition and transfer. Consequently, scientists have developed various techniques to produce CDs with different properties for specific applications. Targeted surface functionalization of CDs has received considerable attention in order to achieve specific requirements in practical applications [28–30]. MOFs are a class of porous crystalline materials carefully constructed from metal nodes and organic ligands through a self-assembly process. MOF exhibit distinctive characteristics, including a vast array of structures, expansive surface areas, adjustable pore sizes, and customizable chemical compositions. These attributes have prompted significant endeavors to functionalize the majority of MOF materials, biomolecules, nanofibers, and polymers by combining MOF with other functional substances, such as nanoparticles (NPs) and CDs [31–33]. This functionalization represents an essential avenue for broadening the scope of MOF-based materials in that MOF composites often display improved, or even previously unknown, properties compared to their native counterparts. However, the functionalization of MOFs with other types of MOFs is still a demanding and comparatively uncharted area [34]. Therefore, it is of great significance to combine MOFs with CDs to stabilize the structure of CDs and enhance the optical properties [35]. Based on these advantages, this study aims to prepare a novel nanomaterial of GCDs@Eu-MOF for different environments, which can be used to detect all FQs rapidly, environmentally friendly and safe accurately and sensitively, and to realize real-time on-site determination of FQs in actual samples.

This paper presents a rapid, accurate, and sensitive method for the detection of FQs based on Eu-MOF-coated citric acid GCDs [36,37]. Synthesized probe materials and synthesis methods are non-hazardous. The synthesis of CDs was conducted via microwave-assisted method using citric acid and urea, and the resulting product was encapsulated in Eu-MOF, synthesized in N-N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) using Europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O) as precursor to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe emitting red-green light. The detection of FQs is achieved through the specific binding of amino groups on the surface of GCDs, which form the carboxyl group on the FQs surface forming an amide bond [38]. This process is facilitated by sensitization effects and energy transfer, which result in green light destruction and red light turning on. This structure markedly enhances the stability of CDs citrate and intensifies the fluorescence intensity of CDs citrate, thereby rendering the phenomenon more pronounced and the reaction more sensitive and precise in the detection FQs. Relying on this micro detection technology for FQs, we can perform trace detection of FQs in different environments, which can establish a regulatory platform for analyzing antibiotic residues in the environment, reduce the continuous impact of chemical companies on the environment, and promote sustainable development.

EuCl3·6H2O, CH4N2O (Urea, Ur), citric acid monohydrate (CA), C2H5OH, NaOH, 3,5-Dicarboxyphenylboronic acid (BBDC), DMF, sparfloxacin (SPX), levofloxacin (LVX), gatifloxacin (GAT), ciprofloxacin (CIP), fleroxacin (FLE), enrofloxacin (ENX), palofloxacin (PLX), lomefloxacin (LOM), marbofloxacin (MBF), oxytetracycline (OXY), gentamicin (GEN), cefadroxil (CEO), cefaclor (CEC), roxithromycin (ROX), clindamycin (CLI), lincomycin (LIN), piperacillin (PIP), vancomycin (VAN), kanamycin (KAN), were supplied by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co in Shanghai. All analytical grade reagents were used. All high purity waters (18.2 MΩ cm−1) used in the experiments were obtained from Millipore instruments.

The microstructure of all materials was examined using a JEM2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM), while a Microtrac ZETA-Check zeta potential analyzer was used to characterize the zeta potentials. The morphology of the Eu-MOF probe was recorded using a Zeiss Sigma scanning electron microscope (SEM). The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were acquired on a powder X-ray diffractometer (Paralytical, Pert PRO) with Cu Kα radiation. A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to acquire the fluorescence spectra. The study used the Malvern 2000 Laser Particle Sizer (DLS). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was measured on an TGA/NETZSCH STA 449C analyzer at a heating rate of 10 ℃/min under nitrogen atmosphere. Brunner-Emmet-Teller surface area and pore size were determined by ASAP 2460 Specific Surface and Pore Size Analyzer. 3D printer (MakerBot Replicator 2X). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy was conducted on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence photographs were captured using a digital camera (Canon EOS 700D) with 320 nm AGL-9406 UV light. A smartphone (Xiaomi 14) was employed for data gathering and the detection of real samples. HPLC grade methanol was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

First, 3 g CA and 1.5 g Ur were soluble in deionized water (20 ml) and mixed for 3 min. The obtained sample was then microwaved (750 W) for 1.5 min. Once the deionized water had fully evaporated, the resulting powder was identified as crude GCDs. This powder was then dissolved in deionized 50 ml water to create a dark brown solution, and then put into a dialysis bag (2000 Da molecular weight of the cut-off) and dialysis for 2 days with ultra-pure water. The final solution of light green GCDs was successfully obtained. The synthesis of GCDs using this method is green and harmless. The resultant solution is to be stored in a fridge at 4 °C for later use.

Eu-MOF was produced by a straightforward solvothermal process, as previously reported. The EuCl3·6H2O (10 mmol L−1) and BBDC (10 mmol L−1) were mixed in violent stirring for 15 min in a DMF/H2O (The volume ratio is 1:1) solution. The resulting solution was then subjected to 16 h of heating at 160 °C in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor. The product obtained was returned to room temperature, centrifuged and thoroughly washed three times with DMF and ethanol. Subsequently, the sample was dried at room temperature in a vacuum oven, and a white powder Eu-MOF was formed. The white powder obtained will be stored in a freezer cabinet at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The obtained Eu-MOF powder (1 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of GCDs solution. The mixture solution was stirred at a rate of 300 r/min for one hour at 50 °C, to give rise to graphite carbon dots@europium metal–organic frameworks (GCDs@Eu-MOF). The GCDs@Eu-MOF probe was obtained as a pale green solution and kept at room temperature in the absence of light. The resulting solution is to be stored in a freezer cabinet at 4 °C or subsequent experiments.

To develop a fluorescence detection strategy for FQs, we employed the use of GCDs@Eu-MOF composite nanoprobes, which were prepared through a process of mixing GCDs and Eu-MOF. Initially, the GCDs@Eu-MOF solution (20 μL) was diluted with ultrapure water so that the final volume of 1990 μL. Following thorough mixing, the obtained solution was moved to a quartz cuvette in a dark environment. Subsequently, 10 μL of different concentrations of FQs (0–1.4 μM) were added to the solution and mixed with ultrapure water. Following a brief period of latency in a dark environment at 25 °C, the fluorescence signal of the solution at λex = 320 nm was recorded with a fluorescence spectrometer in the range of 380 to 800 nm. Fluorescence photographs were taken simultaneously for different concentrations of reactants. To ensure the accuracy of the results, all sensitivity and selectiveity measurements were performed at least three times.

A series of interference signals (OXY, GEN, CEO, CEC, ROX, CLI, LIN, PIP, VAN, and KAN) were mixed with a ratiometric probe to determine the selectiveity of the probe. In the actual test, 20 μL GCDs@Eu-MOF, 1970 μL ultra-pure water and 1.4 μM interfering substance (10 μL) were gradually added. The solution was allowed to react for one minute and then transferred to a quartz cuvette. A fluorescent spectrophotometer was used to record the spectrum of the fluorescence of the ratio probe at a fixed wavelength (320 nm). All measurements of response and specificity were performed in three duplicates. Color changes at 320 nm were monitored under UV light. A fluorescent image of the probe solution was captured at each concentration. The interfering substance concentration was 1.4 μM. Additional 10 μL 1.4 μM FQs were added to the probe solution for interference test, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded again.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of FQs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of these approaches and the usefulness of intelligent sensors in real world environments, we selected lake water, milk, and eggs for FQs residues. Lake water was obtained from the local lochs and milk and eggs were purchased from local supermarkets. The lake water was filtered through a micropore membrane of 0.22 μm for removal of impurities. Before starting the experiment, the eggs and milk had to undergo a preliminary treatment. First, the milk samples were extracted and defatted with hexane and ether. The skimmed milk is then diluted and rinsed with water to remove any polymeric substances that may contaminate the milk. Then, after centrifuging at 8,000 rpm, the supernatant was collected for 3 min. After the eggs were washed with water, the supernatant was centrifuged for 5 min at 10,000 rpm. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a micropore membrane of 0.22 μm for removal of impurities. FQs were added at 0.1, 0.5, and 1 μM final concentrations, followed by fluorescent detection as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A study of the design principle and mechanism of probes
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the structural configuration of GCDs@Eu-MOF and the sensing mechanism for visual detection of FQs. GCDs were synthesized from CA and Ur under microwave irradiation at 750 W. The fluorescence data for excitation and emission of GCDs and GCDs@Eu-MOF were subjected to analysis. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the GCDs fluorescence emission were concentrated at 500 nm. Following doping with Eu-MOF, the fluorescence intensity remains unaltered; however, the Eu-MOF produces a fluorescence emission peak at 690 nm. Given that the optimal excitation wavelengths of GCDs and Eu-MOF are distinct, we selected 320 nm as the excitation wavelength for the probe, as this wavelength allows for the clear observation of the green color and the discernible presence of the red color. The initial color of the hybrid probe is green, which is a consequence of the aggregation of GCDs on the surface of Eu-MOF. This leads to a reduction in the intensity of the red fluorescence of Eu-MOF in water. Following the incorporation of FQs into the probe solution, a reaction occurred between the amino group on the surface of GCDs and the carboxyl group on the benzene ring of FQs occurred via amidation, forming amide-bond structure. This resulted in the disappearance of the green fluorescence of GCDs. Concurrently, the fluorescence absorption peak of Eu-MOF at 690 nm was activated due to the transferred of energy from the added FQs to Eu-MOF and the sensitization of Eu-MOF by FQs. Therefore, for the detection of FQs, the fluorescence changes of GCDs@Eu-MOF could be used as a reported signal. Upon FQs addition, the green fluorescence at 500 nm is quenching and the red light at 690 nm is opening, leading to a clear transition from green to yellow to red. Furthermore, as the concentration of FQs increases, the different colors of fluorescence observed following the reaction allow for a quantitative and sensitive visual detection of FQs. In comparison to the conventional single emission wavelength detection approach, this methodology markedly enhances the detection efficiency and sensitivity. Moreover, due to the ability of the amino group on the GCDs surface to bind with the carboxyl group of FQs, this hybrid probe can be used to detect the complete class of FQs in a quantitative manner with sensitivity and efficiency.

The synthesized GCDs and Eu-MOF were structurally characterized by TEM and SEM, and it can be clearly seen from HRTEM (Fig. S2) that the crystal plane spacing of GCDs is 0.22 nm, which shows good crystallinity. Fig. 1 A shows that average particulate size of the GCDs was around 5 nm, which agrees with the data in the literature as well as the measured DLS data (Fig. S3). Fig. 1B illustrates that Eu-MOF is a parallel hexahedral structure with uniform dimensions and a stable shape. The length, width, and height are approximately 100 nm. The solution of Eu-MOF and GCDs was stirred at 50 °C for one hour. Subsequently, GCDs were adsorbed and aggregated on the Eu-MOF, resulting in the red fluorescence of Eu-MOF being masked and the fluorescence color of the mixed probe solution being green. This indicates that the amount of GCDs affects the fluorescence of Eu-MOF, and the mixing ratio of GCDs and Eu-MOF affects the final fluorescence of the mixed probe solution. Fig. 1D shows that the UV–Vis data exhibit a clear spike at 420 nm, which shifted to 465 nm (Δσ = 45 nm) after the addition of FQs. The observed peak wavelength shift indicates the formation of amide-bond, which suggests that following the addition of FQs, an amide-bond is formed between the FQs and GCDs, while sensitizing Eu-MOF.

The FT-IR spectra of the GCDs @ Eu-MOF and the GCDs @ Eu-MOF + FQs are shown in Fig. 1E. The peak at 1650 cm−1 is a –CO-NH- bond. The shift of the peak from 1650 cm−1 to 1620 cm−1 shows that the carboxy and amino groups are bonded, forming an amide-bond. The absorption peak at 1450 cm−1 was nearly gone after adding FQs, that is, the characteristic absorption peak of amino groups on GCDs disappeared. Thus, it can be concluded that the GCDs show that the amino group around the FQs is bonded to the carboxyl group of the FQs to form an amide-bond. To verify the accuracy of the results we also examined the infrared spectral data plots of Eu-MOF + FQs and GCDs + FQs, respectively, as shown in Fig. S4.

In addition, XPS spectra were further evaluated to confirm the chemical structure and state of the GCDs@Eu-MOF elements. Fig. 1F depicts the complete scan spectrum of the hybrid probe. The inset illustrates the distinctive absorption peaks of europium observed in this spectral region, with two peaks at 1134 eV and 1138 eV, indicating the successful synthesis of Eu-MOF. The high-resolution specific spectra are in Fig. S5. Furthermore, the XRD data for Eu-MOF, as illustrated in the accompanying Fig. 1G, is in accordance with the data presented in the existing literature, thereby substantiating the successful synthesis of Eu-MOF. With the purpose of determine the specific composition of Eu-MOF, we also performed thermogravimetric analysis and the results are shown in Fig. 1H. The rapid decrease in mass at 100°C is due to evaporation of water, after which the rapid decrease in mass of the MOF after 350°C demonstrates combustion of the C element within it. After 600°C, the mass stabilizes, indicating that the remaining elements are non-combustible (such as Eu) which determines the composition of the MOF. The Fig. S6 illustrates the zeta potential of each substance involved in the reaction. The MOF exhibits the highest absolute value of zeta potential, indicating that it is the most stable. The change in zeta potential after the reaction indicates that charge transfer has occurred and the reaction system is more stable, which is consistent with the proposed mechanism.

Additionally, the specific surface area of Eu-MOF was determined (Fig. 1I). The specific surface area of Eu-MOF is 130 m2/g. The findings indicate that Eu-MOF possesses an adequate specific surface area to effectively interact with GCDs and function as an agglomerator of FQs when content is minimal, thereby accelerating the reaction and enhancing the stability of its detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of FQs using GCDs@Eu-MOF probe
ce:section.ce:para: To make this ratiometric fluorescent probe more stable, we also performed a series of measurements on it as follows at pH levels between 4 and 10 (Fig. S7) and at varying temperatures (Fig. S8). At pH values between 4 and 10, the probe demonstrated minimal sensitivity to pH fluctuations, and pH 7 was identified as the optimal pH for the assay. As illustrated in the figure, while the stability of the hybrid probe is diminished at temperatures exceeding 60 °C, the assay conditions typically do not reach this temperature. The data demonstrate that the probe is not affected within the normal temperature range is 4–50 °C. Therefore, the test was conducted at room temperature. The performance of the probes was found to remain stable within 24 h of synthesizing the hybrid probes (Fig. S9). The data indicate that the GCDs@Eu-MOF material has excellent stability and can be used as a probe material for routine detection of FQs. In this paper, we present, for the first time, a sensitive multichromatic response-ratio fluorescence sensing method for the detection of a whole class of FQs with a wider range, good sensitivity, and short response time.

As shown in Fig. S10, when GCDs and Eu-MOF were added at 1: 1, the green fluorescence of the sensor system was evident, and the red fluorescence was not particularly weak, so that the visual inspection could be obtained. At the same time, a continuous and distinct color change from green to red can be seen clearly in the fluorescent photograph. For this reason, a 1: 1 ratio of GCDs to Eu-MOF is selected as the optimum condition to obtain the best visual inspection effect. After confirming the optimal test result, we optimized the detecting condition of the sensor. In addition, a further experiment was carried out to determine the response of the probe to FQs in solution, and the outcome of which is contained in the annex video S1. The response time was observed to be under 1 s, and it was observed that the phenomenon was significant. Fig. 2 illustrates the fluorescence detection of FQs based on the GCDs@Eu-MOF sensing platform (λex = 320 nm). In the absence of FQs, the fluorescence emission occurs at 500 nm (green fluorescence) and 690 nm (red fluorescence). The intensity of the green fluorescence is greater than that of the red fluorescence, and the overall green color of the probe solution is discernible to the unaided eye at this stage. When the FQs concentration is increased from 0 to 1.4 µM, the intensity of the green fluorescence at 500 nm decreases dramatically, eventually causing the probe's green fluorescence to be switched off and the 690-nm red fluorescent light to turn on.

The I690/I500 Fluorescence Intensity Ratio of GCDs @ Eu-MOF showed a strong two-step linear correlation between FQ concentration and R2 between 0.9751 and 0.9945. The mean limit of determination (LOD) is 4.3 nM (Fig. 3 ). The above LOD was calculated as follows: The LOD was calculated as follows: LOD = 3σ/k [39], where σ is a blank signal (n = 9), and k is a slope of the calibration plot. This LOD is below the level of a number of detection methods available at present (Table S1). It is also worth noting that the method exhibits a consistent detection trend across the whole class of FQs. In addition, the whole reaction process was completed in less than 1 s. Compared to other methods, this method demonstrates increased sensitivity and speed, while maintaining a similar visual and detection effect for the whole class of FQs. In this system, as the FQs concentration increases, the fluorescent color is continuously changed from green to orange to yellow to red. Compared with conventional single-emission sensor probes, the proportional fluorescence probe avoids the interference caused by the outside environment and eliminates the need for calibration, thus facilitating more accurate detection results.

In Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence spectra of the sensor probe in the absence of various significant interferences, including representative species of antibiotics (OXY, GEN, CEO, CEC, ROX, CLI, LIN, PIP, VAN, KAN) in which residues may be present, are observed. We can see that there is almost no significant change in fluorescence intensity with the addition of these interfering analytes, and only with the addition of FQs is there a green fluorescence quenching and a red fluorescence turn on. The fluorescence intensity seems to change significantly when compared to I690/I500. This indicates that it has excellent selectiveity for FQs. Furthermore, all substances combined do not interfere with the detection of FQs by the sensor system, and the sensitivity and selectiveity of visual detection is further confirmed by the fluorescence photographs in the inset. (Fig. 4A). In order to investigate the characteristics of interference testing, the interference experiment was added. Specific results are shown in Fig. 4B, when other interfering substances co-existed with FQs, the reaction was not affected. The results indicate that the FQ is highly selective and can be used to detect interference.

In the past, the most of test strip-based techniques were restricted to semi-quantitative monitoring, that relied on the naked eye's ability to detect subtle color variations. Modern rapid detection technology is unable to meet the demand for precise quantifiable control. In the majority of instances, the fluorescence change requires the use of hand-held ultraviolet lamps and dark ambient conditions, making it challenging to achieve portability and real-time detection in the field. In order to solve this problem, we have developed a portable optical sensor platform, which is made up of a removable smart phone, 3D printing technology and an application for color recognition (RGB). A 3D version of the fittings was drawn using the relevant programmer and the specifications were entered into the 3D printer. Fig. S11 illustrates the composition of a handheld needle. The number of FQ may be determined by changing the color of the solution in a hand-held needle. It can also be combined with the device in Fig. S12 for visualization of quantitative detection of FQs. Fig. S12 illustrates the major components of the accessory, a mobile telephone stand, a socket for storing a small USB lamp, and a sample container. The device works in conjunction with a mobile phone to provide a robust and dependable working environment. The mobile phone-based fluorescence detection system for FQs is performed in three parts: imaging, signaling and quantitative evaluation. Exposure of the sample container to different concentrations of FQs results in rapid changes in fluorescence color (Fig. 5 A). As shown in Fig. 5B, the application rapidly captures a fluorescent color photo and converts it to an appropriate RGB value (Fig. 5C). The plasma concentrations are clearly displayed in the Fig. 5D. In this case, the Green/Red Channel (G/R) exhibits an excellent linear relationship with the FQs concentration between 0 and 1.4 μM, and the linear coefficient R1 2 = 0.9862, R2 2 = 0.9794. Thus, the FQs can be quantitatively determined using the G/R value at 4.9 nM. The results show that this simple, portable and real time detecting platform can realize the ultrasonic detection of FQs, and it can be used to protect public health and food safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FQs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess evaluate t the viability and repeatability of this method, a non-standard addition procedure was employed to determine the FQs in actual specimens with a fluorescence ratiometric sensor. The water of lakes was procured from a lake close by, and the milk and eggs were purchased from a local shop. The results of the three samples after pre-treatment are shown in Table 1 . The recovery rate was 96.0 % ∼ 108 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.97 % ∼ 3.66 % when adding 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 μM FQs. The results show that the method is accurate and reliable. These results indicate that a graduated sensor is suitable for use in water and food samples and for the detection of risks due to residue limits in the environment.

In conclusion, we constructed rate sensitive fluorescence micro detection device for FQs using citrate GCDs and Eu-MOF. Upon addition of FQs, the red fluorescence of Eu-MOF turned on slightly, while the GCDs showed a gradient fluorescence burst due to the chemically interaction of the amino group and the carboxyl group on the benzene ring of FQs to form an amide-bond structure, leading to a variety of fluorescence colors from green to red, demonstrating high responsivity with a detection limit of 4.3 nM. The aforementioned findings were employed in the design of a fluorescent sensing needle for the sensitive and rapid micro detection of fluoroquinolones (FQs) utilizing fluorescence color discrimination, in situ and visualized monitoring. Furthermore, a ratiometric dual-emission probe has been successfully implemented in a smartphone sensing platform, thereby validating the potential for the quantitative identification of FQs. The devised apparatus is economically viable, portable and dependable for the analysis of FQs in foodstuffs and the environment, avoids sustainable harm to humans and the environment from FQs. This research optimizes strategies for portable and efficient devices to develop an eco-friendly sensing platform for environmental analysis, food safety and public safety monitoring.
Author Contributions.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. The authors declare no conflict of interest.

This work is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000202), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085 MB30), Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Agricultural Sensors of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, and Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment of Anhui Province. (KLAS2022KF001).

Ruijie Tang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Lingfei Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology. Yaowei Zhu: Data curation. Minghui Zhan: Data curation. Jun Zhao: Data curation. Changlong Jiang: Investigation. Bianhua Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113260.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
